nodes.text: The shelter is a six-by-six post-and-beam square with a corrugated metal roof and three picnic tables under it. Two tables are bolted to the slab. The third is loose, set off-square, like somebody had been planning to make off with it and changed their mind.
On the nearest table, somebody has carved into the wood with what looks like a key:

F + B   1994 — and beside it, in different hand and earlier wood, J. and someone has filled in the rest of the name with a knife and then someone else has scratched the rest back out, leaving J. alone with an unfinished hyphen.
Below those: a small careful sanderling, the same shorebird the Smolvud brochure has on its cover. Whoever drew it loved the bird.

The shelter floor is concrete and dry. Cigarette butts swept toward the corner without being picked up. A flattened pack of Camel Lights, the soft kind. A bottle cap from a Henry Weinhard's root beer.

On the middle table, a paperback face-down, splayed, the spine cracked. The cover faces away. You turn it over with two fingers. It is a copy of GREAT EXPECTATIONS, the Bantam paperback, the schoolbook edition. Somebody has dog-eared one page and left it; somebody has, in pencil, underlined a single sentence about waiting. You do not memorize the sentence. You will think you remember it later and you will be wrong about it.
A pair of sunglasses on the bench, lenses snapped, one ear missing. Not anyone's. Just left.
